Title: 10,000 jobs on offer at Perkeso carnival
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A total of 10,000 job opportunities, with salaries ranging from RM3,000 to RM16,000, were offered at the two-day Social Security Organisation's (Perkeso) MYFutureJobs Premium Carnival 2026, which concluded today.
Human Resources Minister Datuk Seri R. Ramanan said the carnival featured a wide range of employers, including multinational corporations, government-linked companies (GLCs) and private sector firms.
"This is a very good opportunity, especially given the current economic situation, as it offers a wide range of job openings.
"There are 10,000 vacancies available, with salaries ranging from RM3,000 to RM16,000.
"This carnival will also be held in Borneo, expanding access to employment nationwide," he said after visiting the MYFutureJobs Premium Career Carnival 2026 at the Kuala Lumpur Convention Centre today.
Also present was Perkeso chief executive officer Datuk Seri Mohammed Azman Aziz Mohammad.
Ramanan said sectors with high demand include manufacturing and services, as well as nursing, banking and auditing.
"The services sector accounts for nearly 30 per cent of the job opportunities offered, reflecting positive growth in the industry," he said.
He added that the ministry, through Perkeso, has introduced the MyFutureJobs Career Exploration Centre, powered by artificial intelligence (AI), now operating at KL Sentral.
"The centre helps job seekers build profiles, match with suitable jobs and improve their chances of securing employment."
He said the ministry aims to achieve at least 200,000 job placements this year through various initiatives, including MyFutureJobs.
Meanwhile, Ramanan said the ministry will launch a series of targeted crisis support initiatives to address the impact of global economic uncertainty stemming from ongoing geopolitical tensions.
He said the move follows a worrying trend in the labour market, which saw 5,900 Malaysians laid off in March, with workers in Selangor and Kuala Lumpur the hardest hit.
"As a proactive measure by the Madani government, the ministry will announce several comprehensive crisis support initiatives in conjunction with Labour Day on May 1," he said in a statement.
Ramanan added that the initiatives aim to enhance the marketability of job seekers through more dynamic interventions, including improvements to the Employment Insurance System Act.
"These improvements are expected to provide more comprehensive benefits to workers, including stronger social protection and easier access to reskilling and upskilling programmes.
"This effort is a critical step by the ministry to accelerate the redeployment of workers into more stable employment sectors, thereby reducing pressure on the national labour market," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Remand extended for independent preacher suspected of NGO funds abuse
Article:
SEREMBAN: The Negri Sembilan Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) has obtained a remand extension against an
independent preacher
on suspicion of embezzling funds from a non-governmental organisation (NGO) totalling about RM12.3 million.
The five-day remand order until May 1 against the suspect, who is in his 30s, was issued by magistrate Nurul Saqinah Rosli at the magistrate's court here today.
The application to extend the remand was made after the original remand period for the suspect expired today.
Meanwhile, Negri Sembilan MACC director, Awgkok Ahmad Taufik Putra Awg Ismail, when contacted, confirmed the matter and that the case is being investigated under Section 23 of the MACC Act 2009.
It was previously reported that the state MACC had arrested an independent preacher suspected of abusing power and embezzling funds from an NGO totalling about RM12.3 million for personal gain.
MACC Chief Commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki yesterday had said that the commission would call 14 to 15 witnesses to assist in the investigation into the embezzlement of NGO funds belonging to the independent preacher.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Timeline: How the shooting at the White House correspondents' dinner unfolded
Article:
WASHINGTON: A gunman fired shots in the hotel hosting the White House correspondents' association dinner on Saturday, causing President Donald Trump and his cabinet to be rushed out before the suspect was taken into custody.
Here is a timeline of the incident and its immediate aftermath:
Around 8.35pm ET (0035 GMT on Sunday),
guests at the Washington Hilton dinner took cover when shots were heard, video footage showed. Soon after, Secret Service agents rushed towards Trump, escorting the president and his cabinet from the ballroom.
A sole gunman had rushed a Secret Service checkpoint in a lobby, Washington, DC, Mayor Muriel Bowser and interim police chief Jeffery Carroll later told reporters.
The suspect charged the Secret Service checkpoint in the hotel's lobby area and was armed with a shotgun, a handgun and multiple knives, Carroll said. As the suspect ran through that checkpoint, Secret Service members intercepted him.
A Secret Service agent was injured and transported to a local hospital, the mayor said, adding the suspect was also transferred to a local hospital where he was evaluated.
"We do know that law enforcement exchanged gunfire with the individual. ... The suspect in this case, he was not struck by gunfire," Carroll said. "Law enforcement ... they actually tackled him into the ground and handcuffed him."
[[nid:1426519]]
Around 9.17pm ET (0117 GMT),
Trump made his first comments after the shooting, saying law enforcement "acted quickly and bravely." Trump posted on Truth Social that "the shooter has been apprehended." Trump said he recommended that the event continue.
About 20 minutes later,
Trump posted again saying law enforcement asked him and others to leave the premises of the venue and that he was complying with that request. The event will be rescheduled "within 30 days," Trump said.
Around 10.30pm ET (0230 GMT),
Trump addressed the media in a press conference at the White House, accompanied by the director of the FBI and the acting US attorney general. Trump and other law enforcement officials said preliminary information suggested the suspect was a lone shooter.
Around 11.13pm ET (0313 GMT)
, Jeanine Pirro, the US Attorney for the District of Columbia, told reporters the suspect was being charged with using a firearm during a crime of violence and assault on a federal officer with a dangerous weapon.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police hunt mother over alleged abuse of three-year-old in Batu Caves
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police are tracking down a woman suspected of abusing her three-year-old son in an incident in Batu Caves recently.
Gombak police chief ACP Noor Ariffin Mohamad Nasir said a report was lodged yesterday by the child's grandmother, who lives in the same house.
Preliminary investigations found the incident occurred in early March around 8pm, when the complainant heard the child crying and suspected he was being abused by his mother.
"The suspect is a local woman and the child's biological mother. She is not married, and the motive is still under investigation," he said in a statement today.
The child has been taken for a medical examination, which is ongoing.
Police are actively tracing the suspect to assist in investigations. The case is being investigated under Section 31(1)(a) of the Child Act 2001.
Those with information are urged to contact investigating officer Inspector Amirul Ashyraf Abu Hassan at 019-3491233 or the Gombak district police headquarters operations room at 03-61262222.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'I've studied assassinations': Trump muses on reasons for latest shooting
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump struck a philosophical tone Saturday after a shooter rushed a media gala in Washington, the latest in a series of incidents to target the Republican leader.
"I ask respectfully, why do you think this keeps happening to you?" a reporter asked Trump after the chaotic incident at the White House Correspondents' Dinner.
Trump had a ready answer, and it was about the 79-year-old's own position in the pantheon of US presidents.
"Well, you know, I've studied assassinations," Trump said. "And I must tell you the most impactful people, the people that do the most – you take a look at Abraham Lincoln... the people that make the biggest impact, they're the ones that they go after."
"I hate to say I'm honoured by that, but I've done a lot," Trump added. "We've changed this country, and there are a lot of people that are not happy about that. So I think that's the answer."
Trump, who survived an assassination attempt at an election rally in 2024, added that "I lead a pretty normal life, considering, you know, it's a dangerous life."
"A lot of other people, you know, you read stories where they become basket cases. To be honest, I'm not a basket case."
Trump meanwhile took a measured tone towards the press – despite previously calling it the "enemy of the people" – saying there was "a tremendous amount of love and coming together" after the incident.
He said he had been ready to give the "most inappropriate speech ever made" but would now be "very boring" when the dinner was rescheduled.
The shooting on Saturday, which Trump blamed on a "would-be assassin," also caused him to riff on one of his favourite topics – the huge US$400 million ballroom he is building at the White House.
Trump said the venue for the correspondents' dinner, the Washington Hilton, was "not particularly secure" and showed the need for the construction of the new ballroom.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: What we know about the Trump press gala shooting
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump and other attendees were evacuated Saturday night after gunshots were fired at a glitzy media gala in Washington.
As details continue to emerge and investigations are underway, here is what we know about the shooting:
What we know about the ballroom
Shots were heard after the welcoming speech at the annual dinner of the White House correspondents' association being held at the Washington Hilton Hotel, according to AFP reporters and other witnesses.
Tactical security teams with guns drawn took position on the stage where Trump was seated alongside his wife Melania, Vice President JD Vance and other officials, who were swiftly evacuated.
Hundreds of guests in black tie in the ballroom took cover under tables and later made their way into the hotel lobby and then outdoors as the event was postponed.
Authorities said no dignitaries or gala guests were hurt.
How the shooting unfolded
A "sole gunman" rushed through a security checkpoint in the hotel lobby just outside the ballroom where the dinner was taking place around 8:36 pm (0036 GMT), according to authorities.
Trump shared footage on his Truth Social platform which appeared to show the suspect charging at the checkpoint before being swarmed by officers.
"He was armed with a shotgun, a handgun and multiple knives," Metropolitan Police Department interim chief Jeffery Carroll told reporters.
Law enforcement exchanged gunfire with the suspect and "intercepted that individual."
A uniformed Secret Service officer was "struck in his vest" and taken to hospital, but was doing well, Carroll said.
The suspect was not hit by gunfire, but was taken to a hospital to be evaluated.
He was in custody and due to be arraigned on Monday in a federal court.
A long gun and shell casings were found on the scene, FBI Director Kash Patel said, adding that the agency was carrying out witness interviews as part of its probe.
[[nid:1426505]]
Who is the suspect?
Trump shared photos of the suspected shooter, shirtless and in handcuffs facedown on a carpeted floor, in what appears to be the Hilton lobby.
Authorities are yet to publicly confirm his identity, but US media reported that the suspect was a 31-year-old named Cole Tomas Allen from Torrance, California.
Based on preliminary information, "we do believe he was a guest here at the hotel," Carroll told reporters.
The detained man is believed to be the only suspect in the case, officials said.
He is being charged with two counts currently: using a firearm during a crime of violence, and assault on a federal officer using a dangerous weapon, US Attorney Jeanine Pirro said.
Other charges could still be brought as the investigation proceeds.
"My impression is he was a lone wolf," Trump said, adding that the suspect's motivation was not yet clear but he believed the gunman was "sick."
[[nid:1426504]]
Were there security failures?
Questions swirled regarding the security at the reception and how a gun was brought into the hotel.
Attendees pointed out that there was a magnetometer placed outside the ballroom, but there was no such screening before that or at the entrance to the hotel itself.
Trump initially said it was "not a particularly secure building," but later said the ballroom where the event was being held was not breached by the gunman and was "very, very secure."
The checkpoint that the suspect tried to charge past was "right outside the ballroom," authorities said.
"Because that checkpoint worked, there was no one who was injured," Pirro said.
"We'll go through video across the hotel to figure out how the gun got in, how it got down here," Carroll added.
According to Trump, the security services did a "much better job than Butler," where he was the target of an assassination attempt in 2024 during a campaign rally in the state of Pennsylvania.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTViral: Woman calls out diners for smoking in eatery [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A viral video has surfaced showing a woman calling out several individuals for smoking and leaving cigarette ash on a restaurant table.
In the
36-second clip
shared by user @araaz_official on Threads, the woman is seen addressing the group while they remain silent.
She questions their lack of civic-mindedness and respect for other patrons.
"Look at that cigarette ash. Are you planning to clean it up or sweep it away? You don't think about that. You have no respect at all," she says.
She also expresses frustration that they chose to eat and leave without considering the mess left behind.
The video shows the woman saying she intends to report the matter to the Health Department for further action.
The individuals do not respond in the clip.
The video has since drawn praise from netizens, many of whom said smoking has no place in a restaurant.
"These people know it's an eatery, yet they still choose to smoke inside.
"They should think about those around them. If they want to smoke after eating, they should do it outside," said user @skullujang.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Consumers are cautious as psychology of spending changes
Article:
LETTERS:
Malaysians are still spending, but the way they spend has changed. The new consumer is more price-sensitive, more selective and more cautious than before.
A familiar label still matters, but it no longer guarantees the sale. A few ringgit may appear small in a policy paper or boardroom discussion, but it is not small inside a monthly household basket.
The difference between two brands of cooking oil, rice, eggs, detergent, milk powder, shampoo or household cleaner becomes meaningful when repeated week after week.
Consumers are therefore comparing more carefully, switching brands more easily and waiting longer for promotions.
Food, personal care, cleaning products, transport and children's needs remain priorities because households cannot avoid them.
For businesses, the message is clear. A premium price now requires a stronger explanation. Brand reputation alone is no longer enough.
Consumers want visible value: better durability, better ingredients, better safety, better service, better convenience or a more trusted promise.
If the difference is only packaging and advertising, the cheaper product will win.
Trust is lost when consumers feel they are being asked to pay more for less. GDP growth may show that the economy is expanding. Inflation data may suggest that price increases are being managed.
Consumer sentiment surveys may point to caution or confidence. But none of these indicators alone can fully explain how Malaysians experience the economy in daily life.
A family does not calculate inflation through an abstract national basket. It calculates inflation through the items it cannot avoid buying.
For policymakers, this is a crucial signal. Malaysians are adapting, but adaptation has a cost.
They are substituting, delaying, downsizing and bargain-hunting. They remain active in the economy, but with more caution and less emotional confidence.
The response, therefore, cannot stop at advising people to spend wisely.
Consumers need stronger purchasing power, fair competition, transparent pricing, better wage prospects, targeted assistance and protection from unfair market practices.
Financial education also needs to reflect how households actually make decisions under pressure.
Malaysians want affordability, but not poor quality. They want value, but not manipulation.
They want trusted brands, but not blind loyalty. They want retailers to understand that every ringgit in the basket now carries more emotional weight.
Companies that offer honest value, affordable pack sizes, clear promotions, reliable quality and respectful communication will earn loyalty.
Malaysia's consumer in 2026 is not weak but wary.
The economy may be resilient, but the psychology of spending has changed. Every purchase has to work harder for its place in the basket.
DR NORAZAH MOHD SUKI
Professor
Marketing and E-Commerce
Universiti Utara Malaysia
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Pontian police seize 18 e-bikes used by foreigners on public roads
Article:
PONTIAN: Police seized 18 micro-mobility vehicles, including electric motorcycles and bicycles, used by foreigners on public roads during a traffic crackdown on Friday.
The operation targeted the illegal use of e-bikes on main roads, with 56 people and 42 vehicles checked at roadblocks in Pekan Nanas, Jalan Utama Taman Seri Maju, and Pontian Jaya.
District police chief Superintendent Hadzrat Hussien Mion said micro-mobility vehicles were restricted from public roads under traffic laws due to safety concerns.
Police seized 18 e-bikes during the operation, while 30 summonses were issued to other motorists for various offences, including driving without a licence, lacking insurance, and having expired road tax.
Police also impounded three motorcycles and a car driven by foreigners without valid licences and insurance, as well as four bicycles ridden at night without lights.
Hadzrat said the operation followed public complaints and ongoing concerns over road safety involving e-bikes.
The use of e-bikes and other personal mobility devices on public roads is prohibited. Offenders face fines of up to RM2,000 or imprisonment of up to six months upon conviction, he said in a statement.
He said the prohibition was necessary as most e-bikes were not registered or insured, and were not designed to meet safety standards for traffic, posing a hazard to riders and other road users.
The cases are being investigated under Section 64 of the Road Transport Act 1987, which allows for vehicle seizures of up to 90 days, and Regulation 3(1) of the Road Traffic (Prohibition of Certain Micro-Mobility Vehicles) Rules 2021.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Get down!' Panic and chaos at glitzy media gala [WATCH]
Article:
WASHINGTON: A glitzy gala for the White House Correspondents' Association was shattered by gunfire on Saturday night, forcing security to rush President Donald Trump offstage as guests dived for cover.
Trump was seated on stage for the annual dinner — his first as president — when loud bangs disrupted the event.
Moments later, cries of "stay down!" echoed through the ballroom as guests in formal attire, including administration officials and cabinet members, took cover.
US Secret Service agents, with weapons drawn, surrounded the president and First Lady Melania Trump before ushering them through a back curtain.
Trump said later he initially "thought it was a tray going down" before realising it was gunfire."It was either a tray or a bullet. I hoped it was a tray, but it wasn't," he said.
DUCKED UNDER THE TABLE
The music stopped and a hush fell over the room as agents swarmed the ballroom of the Washington Hilton.
The hotel is the same site where president Ronald Reagan survived an assassination attempt 45 years ago.
Mehmet Oz, the administrator of the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, said there were "shots fired upstairs" as security evacuated him.
Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. was also rushed out of the room. Kennedy's father, Robert F. Kennedy, and his uncle, president John F. Kennedy, were both killed by assassins in the 1960s.
One America News correspondent Alexandra Ingersoll said she ducked under a table when the commotion began.
"I didn't know if the shooter was neutralised or what was going on," she said.
Trump later told reporters that a gunman, whom he described as a "lone wolf," had charged through a security screening just outside the ballroom.
FOUGHT LIKE HELL
The room was ordered evacuated, and several hundred guests moved into the lobby and out into the street.
Trump's motorcade left the Hilton about 8.40pm and headed for the White House.
The president, still in his black-tie attire, later appeared at a press conference in the White House briefing room alongside Melania Trump, Vice President JD Vance, and FBI director Kash Patel.
Trump, who survived an assassination attempt at a rally in Butler, Pennsylvania, in 2024, said he "fought like hell to stay" at the dinner but was told by agents he had to leave.
He added that the gala would be rescheduled.
"We're going to do it again," Trump said. "We're not going to let anyone take over our society."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US Fed set to hold rates steady again on cost hikes from Mideast war
Article:
WASHINGTON: The US central bank is widely expected to keep interest rates unchanged at its policy meeting next week, as energy prices stay high and supply chains snarled due to war in the Middle East.
The Federal Reserve's two-day meeting, starting Tuesday, could be chairman Jerome Powell's last at the helm of the independent institution.
But it takes place against a tricky backdrop. Powell's successor has faced a bumpy road to confirmation, while policymakers battle competing pressures as steeper fuel prices drive inflation and job market worries linger.
Fed officials are set to keep rates steady at a range between 3.50 per cent and 3.75 per cent, extending their pause since the start of the year.
"We still have a very high level of uncertainty on what's happening in the Middle East," KPMG senior economist Kenneth Kim told AFP.
Oil and gasoline prices remain elevated even if they have peaked, meaning "there's certainly an energy shock that's still impacting both consumers and businesses," he said.
The Fed has a dual mandate of maintaining price stability and low unemployment. It tends to keep interest rates high to curb inflation or lower them to spur growth, meaning that current conditions pull officials in different directions.
Navy Federal Credit Union Chief Economist Heather Long expects Powell to be "non-committal" on the path of rates, as the full impact from the war on Iran remains unknown.
The oil price hikes came after US-Israeli strikes targeting Iran from February 28 sparked Tehran's retaliation in virtually closing the Strait of Hormuz -- a key waterway for energy transit.
- Containing inflation -
Fed officials will likely focus more on containing inflation than the jobs market this meeting, with the war entering its ninth week.
The strait is also a key passage for fertilizers, and disruptions threaten to hit food production.
Already, US consumer inflation reached its highest level in nearly two years in March at 3.3 per cent as energy costs rocketed.
Fed Governor Christopher Waller, who earlier backed lower rates to support employment, indicated this month that a prolonged conflict could make it hard for the central bank to cut rates this year.
If there were high inflation and a weak labor market, one would have to balance risks on both sides.
This "may mean maintaining the policy rate at the current target range if the risks to inflation outweigh those to the labor market," he told an Alabama event.
KPMG's Kim said solid hiring recently "gives the Fed some cushion" to temporarily focus more on prices.
Analysts will monitor if the Fed signals in its post-meeting statement that rate hikes are a possibility.
- 'Critical juncture' -
The Fed is also taking its next steps under intense political scrutiny.
President Donald Trump has made no secret of his wish for lower interest rates, and regularly slammed Powell for not cutting them aggressively.
Beyond rhetoric, Trump has sought to oust Fed Governor Lisa Cook over claims of mortgage fraud. The Supreme Court is set to rule on whether he can fire her.
Meanwhile, Trump's choice of new Fed chairman -- Kevin Warsh -- has faced a bumpy road to confirmation.
Republican senator Thom Tillis on the Senate Banking Committee vowed to block Fed appointments until a Justice Department probe into the Fed and Powell is resolved, setting up a potential impasse on the panel Warsh needs to clear.
But the Department of Justice said Friday it would drop the investigation linked to renovation costs overruns, potentially paving the way for Warsh's ascendance.
Asked by journalists Saturday about the DOJ's move, Trump said he still wants to look into the cost of the Federal Reserve building renovations, which he has claimed is too high.
"I tell you, I want to find out. I have an obligation to find out," he said.
Warsh has repeatedly pledged to remain independent if confirmed.
"We're at a critical juncture for the Fed," EY-Parthenon chief economist Gregory Daco told AFP.
"It may be that under Warsh, we're going to see less Fed transparency, less Fed communication than we had in the past," he said, referring to Warsh's confirmation hearing testimony.
Powell's chairman term expires May 15, and he originally intended to stay on the Fed's board of governors until the probe on him is completed.
All eyes are on his future plans at his scheduled press briefing Wednesday.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump says shooting won't 'deter' him from Iran war
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump said Saturday a shooting at the White House correspondents dinner would not deter him from the Iran war, although he believed the incident was unlikely to be linked to the conflict.
"It's not going to deter me from winning the war in Iran. I don't know if that had anything to do with it, I really don't think so, based on what we know," Trump told reporters in a briefing at the White House after the security incident.
Trump had earlier said, however, that "you never know" if it could be linked to the Iran war, and said investigators were working on the motive of the shooter whom he described as a "lone wolf."
The US leader earlier Saturday cancelled his envoys' trip to Pakistan for peace talks with Iran, after being unimpressed with Teheran's negotiating position following nearly two months of war.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Student, 17, rescued after falling into 7m-deep school well
Article:
TUMPAT: A 17-year-old student sustained head and body injuries after falling into a seven-metre-deep well at his school in Wakaf Bharu here today.
Wakaf Bharu Fire and Rescue Station operations commander Azmi Sulaiman said the department received an emergency call regarding the incident at 9.12am.
"Six firemen were deployed to the scene and arrived 11 minutes later.
"Upon arrival, they found the victim trapped inside the well and immediately initiated a rescue operation using specialised equipment, including ropes and a rescue ladder," he said.
Azmi said firemen took more than 20 minutes to extricate the student from the well.
"The operation was completed around 10am. The victim suffered minor injuries and was sent for treatment," he said.
The victim, identified as Wan Ahmad Mukhlis Wan Md Fadhli, was reportedly sitting on a concrete slab covering the well when the incident occurred.
The slab is believed to have cracked as it could not support the victim's weight, causing him to plunge into the well.
The well was previously used as a groundwater source by the school but has long been abandoned.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Six passengers injured evacuating from Swiss Air flight in India
Article:
BENGALURU: Six passengers were injured and hospitalised after a Swiss Air flight from Delhi to Zurich aborted takeoff and was evacuated on the runway at the Indira Gandhi International Airport early Sunday, the Economic Times reported.
The incident occurred when one of the aircraft's engines failed and caught fire during the takeoff roll, prompting the crew to stop the aircraft and initiate an emergency evacuation, the report said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTviral: Hit-and-run caught on video in Jalan Templer traffic
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A viral video has surfaced showing a hit-and-run incident along Jalan Templer involving a tour bus during heavy traffic yesterday.
The 27-second clip, shared by Mynewshub+ on Facebook, shows a tour bus crashing into a silver Perodua Myvi before driving away.
The post also included images of the Myvi, with its front section severely damaged.
It is unclear whether there were any injuries or fatalities, and whether any arrests have been made in connection with the incident.
The New Straits Times is reaching out to authorities for further information.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fed-up traders call for protests to be held outside city centre
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A group representing hawkers and petty traders has expressed frustration over rallies being held in business hotspots instead of open spaces.
Federation of Malaysian Hawkers and Petty Traders Association president Datuk Seri Rosli Sulaiman said demonstrations in city centres lead to congestion and road closures.
"I am puzzled as to why these must be held in popular areas. Why not hold them elsewhere, such as Padang Merbok or areas that do not disturb the public and traders?" Rosli said.
He said traders are invariably affected whenever demonstrations occur near the Sogo shopping complex in Jalan Tuanku Abdul Rahman, with many reporting a drop in sales.
"People visit the area for various purposes, including buying fabric, jewellery, and daily necessities. When conditions become uncomfortable, they choose not to come," he said.
Yesterday, a commotion broke out at a rally held in protest of Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki after
fireworks
were set off at the gathering site.
Rosli said popular public areas should remain accessible for those wishing to shop and relax.
"Friday, Saturday, and Sunday are the primary trading days when people go out to shop. These days should not be disrupted, especially when our economic situation remains uncertain.
"I hope the authorities will be firmer and ensure this does not happen again," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UUE Holdings RM536mil orderbook offers two-year earnings visibility
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: UUE Holdings Bhd's outstanding orderbook of RM536.4 million is expected to provide more than two years of earnings visibility, RHB Research said.
The research house said the orderbook includes a recently secured RM16 million engineering, procurement, construction and commissioning contract.
"We view this as strategically significant as it signals UUE Holdings' ability to move up the value chain," it said in a note.
Meanwhile, RHB Research said a memorandum of understanding with Asean Cableship involving a potential joint venture for subsea horizontal directional drilling works has lapsed.
However, the firm is not overly concerned, as UUE Holdings can still participate in subsea tenders independently, potentially retaining full profit share compared with the previously proposed 50:50 structure.
UUE Holdings reported fourth-quarter financial year 2026 (FY26) core net profit of RM7.3 million, bringing full-year core net profit to RM22.1 million.
RHB Research said the results exceeded expectations, coming in at 106 per cent of its forecast and 110 per cent of consensus estimates, mainly driven by stronger-than-expected margins.
The research house lowered its FY27 to FY28 earnings forecasts by three per cent and 13 per cent respectively, mainly due to adjustments in orderbook recognition timing.
"That said, we maintain our RM320 million annual contract replenishment assumption, which we believe is achievable given UUE Holdings' strong positioning in Tenaga Nasional Bhd-related projects," it said.
RHB Research maintained its "Buy" call on the stock with a target price of 48 sen.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Rising resin costs to weigh on Southern Cable
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Southern Cable Group Bhd is expected to face margin pressure in the second half of 2026 as rising polyethylene prices weigh on Malaysian cable manufacturers, Hong Leong Investment Bank Bhd (HLIB) said.
The firm said Southern Cable's margins are likely to remain steady in the first half of the year, with any compression skewed towards the second half.
"Southern Cable has polyethylene inventory coverage until June 2026, in line with peers, with the next Tenaga procurement cycle for July to September to be locked in from June onwards.
"This should keep first-half margins stable before higher input costs begin flowing through in the second half of 2026," it said in a note.
HLIB said its sensitivity analysis showed that every one per cent increase in polyethylene prices would trim gross profit margin by about 0.06 percentage point.
It noted that plastic costs for long-term contracts were locked in during 2024 at relatively elevated levels.
"The recent price increases were still below those levels and should moderate margins versus financial year 2025 rather than push the segment into losses," it said.
However, HLIB said a repeat of the 2022 margin squeeze, which saw the company's net margins contract sharply during a high polyethylene price environment, was unlikely.
It said this was due to structurally improved operating conditions.
"In any case, our conservative earnings forecasts provide a buffer if margins erode in the second half of 2026.
"Our back-of-the-envelope calculations suggest polyethylene prices would need to rise by another 10 per cent before challenging our margin assumptions.
"All in, we maintain our Buy call with an unchanged target price of RM2.59," it added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Immigration officer among 10 held in 'Ops Toyu' smuggling syndicate bust
Article:
ALOR STAR: A senior Immigration officer and a Thai agent were among 10 people detained following an operation at the Bukit Kayu Hitam Immigration, Customs, Quarantine and Security (ICQS) complex yesterday.
The 12.50pm operation, dubbed "Ops Toyu", was conducted by a special task force from the Putrajaya Immigration Department's Intelligence and Operations Division, with support from Kedah Immigration and the Border Control and Protection Agency (MCBA).
Immigration officials said the syndicate, which has been active since early this year, is estimated to have caused a RM4.1 million leakage in national revenue.
In a statement, the department said investigations revealed the syndicate's modus operandi involved smuggling prohibited immigrants, primarily from India, across the land border.
A "transporter" posing as a taxi driver would ferry the foreigners from Thailand into Malaysia to evade suspicion.
Upon reaching the border, the senior officer acting as a "facilitator" would endorse the passports despite the individuals failing the entry requirements.
The syndicate allegedly charged RM1,000 per person, with RM400 paid to the officer for every passport endorsed.
During the raid, authorities arrested the senior officer, the Thai agent, two local men, one of whom acted as a transporter, five Indian nationals, and one Pakistani man.
One of the Indian nationals was found to be on a blacklist, while the Pakistani man possessed a suspected forged e-visa.
Officers seized six passports, Malaysian and Thai border passes, an immigration security stamp, 12 mobile phones, and 30,000 Indian Rupees.
A Nissan Sentra and two motorcycles were also impounded.
Three suspects are being investigated under Section 26A of the Anti-Trafficking in Persons and Anti-Smuggling of Migrants (Atipsom) Act 2007, while one local man is held under Section 26J of the same Act.
The foreign nationals are being investigated under the Immigration Act 1959/63 for various offences. Four other locals have been issued notices to assist in the investigation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Immigration busts syndicate using layered transit route via Singapore, Thailand
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Immigration Department has busted an Indonesian migrant smuggling syndicate that used a complex, multi-layered transit route involving Singapore and Thailand to evade Malaysian authorities.
The operation was carried out by officers from the Anti-Trafficking in Persons and Anti-Smuggling of Migrants (Atipsom) and Anti-Migrant Labour Abuse (Amla) Prevention Division at the Immigration Headquarters in Putrajaya.
In a statement, the department said the operation, conducted yesterday, led to the arrest of 10 Indonesian migrants and two transporters, aged between 27 and 60, at Terminal Bersepadu Gombak and Terminal Bersepadu Selatan.
A Mazda CX-5, believed to have been used to transport the migrants, was also seized during the raids. Authorities were alerted to the group's arrival in Klang Valley from Kelantan via express bus.
"Preliminary investigations revealed that the syndicate had shifted its modus operandi from using sea routes in Selangor waters to a more complex, layered approach involving both air and land routes to throw off enforcement agencies.
"The syndicate also avoided direct flights into Malaysia, as many of the migrants involved were believed to have been blacklisted from entering the country," the statement said.
Migrants reportedly travelled from Indonesia to Singapore or via Kuala Lumpur International Airport as transit points before flying to Hat Yai, Thailand.
From Hat Yai, they were moved to transit houses before entering Kelantan through illegal routes in Bukit Bunga, Tanah Merah. Once in Kelantan, the migrants were transported to Kuala Lumpur using various overnight express bus services to avoid detection.
The department added that the syndicate charged each migrant between RM3,500 and RM4,000 to facilitate illegal entry into Malaysia.
"Each agent plays a specific role, including managing migrant arrivals, arranging transit accommodation, and forging entry stamps in passports to mislead authorities.
"This layered network structure is designed to separate and isolate information links between migrants and syndicate members, making detection more difficult," the department said.
The department also believes locals assisted the syndicate with logistics and bus ticket purchases, and is working to track down those involved based on intelligence.
The suspects are being held at an Immigration Detention Depot for investigation under the Act 670 of the Anti-Trafficking in Persons and Anti-Smuggling of Migrants (Atipsom) Act 2007 and Act 155 of the Immigration Act 1959/63.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: China FM tells Myanmar leader Beijing will back its security, sovereignty
Article:
BEIJING:  China's top diplomat Wang Yi said Beijing will "firmly support" Myanmar in safeguarding its national sovereignty and security, in a meeting with junta chief-turned-president Min Aung Hlaing.
Wang has been on a three-country visit to Southeast Asia this week, travelling to Cambodia, Thailand and Myanmar, seeking to strengthen ties in the face of "risks" and present Beijing as a more stable alternative to the United States.
Beijing supports Myanmar "in forging a successful development path that aligns with its national conditions and enjoys the support of its people", Wang told Min Aung Hlaing during a visit to capital Naypyidaw, according to a Chinese foreign ministry readout published late Saturday.
"As this year marks the first year of the new Myanmar government's tenure, both sides should seize this opportunity to carry forward and promote their traditional friendship, (and) open up new prospects for bilateral relations," Wang said.
Min Aung Hlaing was sworn in as president this month, continuing his rule from a civilian post five years after snatching power in a military coup.
The parliamentary ceremony where he was sworn in was attended by representatives including from China, which analysts say was the junta-backed election's biggest supporter.
Democracy watchdogs have described the tightly controlled election that concluded in January as an effort to rebrand military rule, with voting not taking place in swathes of the country controlled by rebels who are fighting the military and rejected the poll.
China, Wang added, was also willing to cooperate with Myanmar to "resolutely and thoroughly eradicate the scourge" of online gambling and telecommunications fraud.
Myanmar has emerged as a hotspot for cyberscam operations in recent years.
Transnational crime groups initially mostly targeted Chinese speakers before widening their reach and stealing tens of billions of dollars annually from victims around the world.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia Smelting prospects bright on tin rally
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia Smelting Corp Bhd is poised for stronger earnings growth amid expectations of elevated tin prices and improving mining operations, Public Investment Bank Bhd (PublicInvest) said.
Following a recent site visit to Malaysia Smelting's Rahman Hydraulic Tin (RHT) open-pit mine, the research house said optimism was also supported by steady gains in operational efficiency and recovery rates.
"Although production costs are expected to increase, largely due to higher diesel prices, this is expected to be offset by higher selling prices.
"Each one-ringgit increase in diesel price is estimated to raise production cost by US$1,000 per tonne, while tin prices are trading at around US$50,000 per tonne," it said.
Meanwhile, PublicInvest said the commissioning of the company's new tin tailing scavenging plant is set to lift daily tin concentrate production to 14 tonnes from 11 tonnes previously.
It said the company is investing RM10 million to install a new rotary furnace at RHT, with completion expected in the third quarter of 2026.
"The furnace will process 10 tonnes of tin per day, enabling on-site conversion of ore into crude tin.
"This will reduce haulage volume, logistics costs and overall transportation complexity, while improving operational efficiency," it said.
PublicInvest maintained its "Outperform" call on Malaysia Smelting with an unchanged target price of RM2.60.
"We maintain our view that the company is in a strategic position to play a more important role in this field should opportunities arise in the future," it added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Simultaneous Melaka, Johor polls could save millions, says analyst
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: Holding the Melaka and Johor state elections simultaneously could save millions in taxpayer funds due to the proximity of the two states, an analyst said.
Universiti Malaya political analyst Dr Mohammad Tawfik Yaakub said combined polls would allow for more organised logistics for both government machinery and political parties.
The Melaka and Johor state assemblies are set to dissolve automatically on Dec 27, 2026, and April 21, 2027, respectively.
By law, the Election Commission (EC) must hold an election within 60 days of a dissolution. This means the Melaka state election must be held by late February 2027, while Johor's must take place by June 2027, should both assemblies reach their full terms.
However, elections could be held simultaneously or in close succession if the assemblies are dissolved around the same time.
"Party machineries would not need to be deployed twice to the south, and party workers and government agencies could perform their duties for both states within a short period," Tawfik said.
He added that supporters and workers from afar would also avoid doubling their travel costs.
Universiti Teknologi Malaysia Perdana Centre associate professor Dr Mazlan Ali shared a similar view, noting that simultaneous elections would help conserve resources during a period of global energy crises.
He said holding elections for a single state involves high costs, whereas the EC could save money if two states went to the polls together.
"If held separately, costs would certainly increase as the EC would need to mobilise its staff and resources on multiple occasions," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Loud bangs and a Trump evacuation: Chaos at correspondents' dinner
Article:
IT was meant to be a glitzy Saturday night at a Washington ballroom featuring President Donald Trump, but the glamour was shattered by gunshots that left guests diving to the floor and the US leader bundled out by security personnel.
Trump was seated on the dais at the White House Correspondents' Association's annual dinner – the first time he is attending as president – when loud bangs disrupted the revelry and caused him and others on stage to look up in alarm.
Video captured by AFPTV, other news outlets, and AFP reporters attending the event showed a chaotic scene unfolding.
Moments after what sounded like gunshots, cries of "Stay down!" and "Get down!" were heard while guests, including correspondents, officials in the Trump administration and some members of his cabinet took cover.
Amid the chaos, the president was quickly surrounded by US Secret Service personnel, their weapons drawn, and they quickly rushed Trump off the stage and through a back curtain as the crowd crouched in shock.
The big band music stopped, and people in ball gowns and tuxedos turned quiet as agents swarmed around tables and over guests on the floor of the massive ballroom of the Washington Hilton, the very hotel where president Ronald Reagan survived an assassination attempt 45 years earlier.
"Shots fired upstairs," said Mehmet Oz, Trump's administrator of the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, as he was being evacuated by security.
Also seen being rushed out of the ballroom was Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F Kennedy Jr – whose uncle, president John F Kennedy, was felled by an assassin's bullet in 1963 in Dallas, Texas.
[[nid:1426441]]
Alexandra Ingersoll, a correspondent for One America News, told AFP she was inside when the commotion began, and saw Secret Service spring into action to protect the president.
"I just ducked under the table and I was like 'I'm not going to risk this,'" she told AFP. "I didn't know if the shooter was neutralised or what was going on."
With still few details on what actually occurred, the entire ballroom was ordered evacuated, and several hundred guests made their way into the Hilton lobby and out into the chilly air.
Guests were seen hugging, making calls, texting friends and loved ones, and their news bureaus.
Later the Secret Service said in a statement that it was investigating a shooting near the main security screening perimeter for the event.
"The president and the first lady are safe, along (with) all protectees," the agency said. "One individual is in custody."
It was not immediately clear whether the assailant fired shots, or security agents responded to the threat with gunfire of their own.
At about 8.40pm (0040 GMT), an AFP reporter saw police officers racing through the streets surrounding the Hilton, lifting barriers, ushering passersby over to different streets, and clearing cars from the area as quickly as possible.
A helicopter circled overhead. A few minutes later, a motorcade left the Hilton, in the direction of the White House.
* The writers are from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man killed, wife and cousin injured in Tenom 4WD crash
Article:
TENOM: A man was killed while his wife and cousin were injured after their four-wheel-drive vehicle skidded and overturned at Km7 of Jalan Tenom-Enubai near Kampung Sri Meranti last night.
The victim, Victor Chung Yin Tat, 41, was pronounced dead at the scene in the 10.20pm incident.
His wife, 37, sustained severe injuries, including broken ribs on her left side, while his 28-year-old cousin suffered head injuries. Both were rushed to Tenom Hospital for treatment.
District police chief Superintendent Azmir Abd Razak said preliminary investigations revealed the Toyota Hilux was travelling from Kampung Bunsilon towards Tenom town.
"The incident occurred on a straight stretch of road, but the surface was wet due to rain and the area was dark.
"The driver is believed to have lost control before the vehicle skidded into the left shoulder of the road and overturned," he said when contacted today.
Azmir said the victim's body was sent to the Tenom Hospital forensics unit before being transferred to Keningau Hospital for a post-mortem.
The case is being investigated under Section 41(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: New parties eye GRS after Usno departure
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: Several political parties have applied to join Gabungan Rakyat Sabah (GRS), reflecting the state's dynamic political landscape, said its secretary-general, Datuk Seri Masidi Manjun.
Masidi, who is also Sabah Deputy Chief Minister II and Finance Minister, said the decision by the United Sabah National Organisation (Usno) to leave GRS was part of the ever-changing and unpredictable political reality in the state.
"People will come and go. But for now, there are already new applications from other political parties. Let the GRS chairman (Datuk Seri Hajiji Noor) announce it at the appropriate time," he told reporters here on Friday.
Masidi was speaking after attending a gala night organised by the Malaysia-China Chamber of Commerce (MCCC) Sabah branch.
Commenting on Usno's departure, he said politics was fluid, and it was often impossible to foresee developments beyond the next day.
"While I wish they had stayed, we respect their decision to quit GRS. I think we part as friends and not as enemies, and that is the most important thing," he added.
GRS information chief Datuk Joniston Bangkuai, who was also present, said he respected the move and described it as a normal occurrence in politics.
"We understand the reason given — that they are no longer compatible. For us in GRS, we will continue to focus on uniting all parties and communities in Sabah. We will not lose our focus," he said.
On Wednesday, Usno president Tan Sri Pandikar Amin Mulia confirmed the party had left the coalition following a meeting with Hajiji.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTviral: Mat Rempit swarm toll plaza in viral video
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A group of motorcyclists turned a highway toll plaza into a scene of chaos early this morning, performing dangerous stunts and obstructing traffic.
The incident was captured in a 44-second video uploaded to X by UpdateInfo (
@update11111
) at 2.41am.
The footage depicts a large swarm of motorcyclists performing various stunts, including "wheelies", directly at the entrance of the plaza.
A large number of riders were also seen parked in front of the toll booths, watching the display.
The reckless antics caused a significant disturbance, with motorcycles circling back and forth as other vehicles attempted to navigate through the toll gates.
The exact location of the incident has yet to be identified. The New Straits Times has reached out to the authorities for further information.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor Umno Youth calls for economic stimulus package amid energy crisis
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Johor Umno Youth has urged the state government to introduce a comprehensive economic stimulus package to cushion the impact of the global energy crisis.
State Umno Youth chief Noor Azleen Ambross said rising fuel costs were squeezing households and businesses across key sectors.
He proposed that the stimulus package prioritise vulnerable groups hardest hit by price hikes, including school and tour bus operators, tourist boat operators in Mersing, and small-scale fishermen and farmers.
Noor Azleen said following the wing's meeting yesterday, it was agreed that the state government and relevant authorities should implement immediate relief measures such as rebates, rental discounts, and reductions in premises taxes to assist small and medium enterprises (SMEs).
The wing also proposed that the State Housing Development Corporation offer rental discounts to tenants as cost-of-living pressures intensify.
Noor Azleen said the state must consider including single individuals under 40 in phase four of Bantuan Kasih Johor, a state financial aid programme for low- and middle-income groups.
"This aid should also include postal workers and security guards as eligible recipients, as the cost of living has increased significantly due to higher fuel prices," he said in a statement today.
On party matters, the youth wing welcomed the
Rumah Bangsa initiative
, which facilitated the return of 6,252 leaders at various levels.
Rumah Bangsa is an Umno-led outreach and reconciliation initiative aimed at reuniting former members and strengthening party ranks across all levels.
Noor Azleen said the initiative had boosted the party's ability to operate effectively by expanding membership, restoring leadership depth, tightening coordination from grassroots to state level, and sharpening its election machinery and outreach.
The party has recorded a surge in new memberships via online registration, particularly among youth, which he said reflected renewed confidence in Umno.
"A statewide campaign to attract more youth members through digital sign-ups will be rolled out soon, while divisions were encouraged to organise events to welcome newcomers and reinforce grassroots machinery," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Shooting suspect was 'would-be assassin,' says Trump [WATCH]
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump said Saturday that the suspect behind a shooting incident at a media gala in Washington was a "would-be assassin," adding that the man was "armed with multiple weapons."
The man charged past a security checkpoint, Trump told a press briefing at the White House, adding: "One officer was shot but saved by the fact that he was wearing a, obviously, a very good bulletproof vest."
"We looked at all of the conditions that took place tonight, and I will say, you know, it's not a particularly secure building," the US leader added.
This is an image, unconfirmed, that I believe to be the body of the shooter at the
@whca
dinner.
Multiple shots were fired at the White House correspondents dinner with president Trump in attendance.
I heard 5-6 shots ring out, shortly before observing a dead body surrounded…

April 26, 2026
